COMBAT
DETERMINE DAMAGE EFFECTS In Mw3. the Damage Factor is not inflicted directly on the character in any way: Instead this amount of damage trans Iated using the Wound Table into the form of nonlethal Fatigue and life-threatening wounds Once the total damage is determined_ reference the Wound Table (p_ 114) to find what the effects of the attack are Find the number in the Damage Factor (DF) column closest to the final damage result the attack, without going over. The corre sponding lists the damage effects for that attack. Fatigue, wound types and other effects are described below.

ARMOR EfFECTS Once the base level of wound is known_ the effects f armor are applied (assuming the character is wearing any). This effect is determined by comparing the armor penetration (API value of the weapon being used (see Equipment Data; 132 and Weapon Table, 153) t0 the armor value (AV) of the target: If the AP is equal to higher than the AV; the armor has no effect and the wound result stands_ If the AV is higher than the AP; each point by which the AV is higher than the AP reduces the wound effect by one level o the table If armor reduces the damage down past the 'Fatigue Only level; the attacknas no effect at all Unless the target's armor reduces the effect below the Fatigue Only level, any successful attack   inflicts point Fatigue on the target, in addition to any wound effects. AP Zero Attacks: Tougher characters are more resistant to attacks with base armor penetration of 0, such as unarmed melee attacks In the case attacks with an AP 0f 0, the Damage Factor is decreased by an amount equal to the target' $ BOD Attribute minus his current Fatigue_ This modifier cannot ower than character with BOD who has suffered 5 Fatigue Or more would subtract nothing; Pointblank Range: Ranged weapons usea pointblank range or melee combat add Modifier t0 their AP value. Extreme Range= Ranged weapons used at extreme range apply Modifier to their AP value, minimum 0f 1.
FATIGUE Fatigue represents the physical and mental exhaustion 0i combat, which can result from attacks against character as well the effort put behind special maneuvers and other aclions made by  the character;  Fatigue represents not only ~tiredness put also the various bumps pruises cuts and scrapes that result from combat; None of these injuries will kill character; but they can wear him down Each time a allack successfui; and the target's armor does not reduce the effect below the Fatigue Only' levei on the Wound Table , the target takes Fatigue point . Other situations that cause Fatigue are stated in the particular rules relating to them;
Armor Value Armor is numerically rated with an armor value (AV) from to 10. The numbers correspond t0 the overall size ad strength of the armor; representing the ability 0f the armor t0 disperse tne force of an attack and reduce its effect against the wearer. Each type of armor has four ratings, corresponding to the four general damage types: melee_ ballistic, energy and explosive- Armor rated at 2/5/1/1 would function as AV against melee attacks against ballistic attacks_ and AV against energy and explosive attacks_
Effects of Fatigue Once the amount of Fatigue the character has suffered exceeds nis WIL; all of his Action Check target numbers increased by the amount of Fatigue he has suffered excess of his WIL For example character with WIL wno has suf- tered Fatigue would have +2 Modifier t0 all of his TNs_ The more Fatigue character suffers the more susceptible t0 being stunned knocked out and knocked down AIso the character becomes more susceptible wounding from unarmed attacks_ and nis Fatigue subtracted from Initiative . These specific effects are fully explained the rele vant sections 0f the rules;
